channel.title: Harry's Vault
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Generations of Currency Crisis
item.description:
Economics scholarship classifies currency crises into 3 generationsA government with a currency peg is spending more than it earns (budget deficit) and prints money to cover this deficit. Attempting to account for the ensuing inflation begins to drain the central bank's foreign reserves until a collapse seems inevitable and investors engage in a Speculative Attack.Example Mexico consistently had large budget deficits in the 70s and 80s. To fund this deficit, they expanded the domestic money supply. Because they were trying to keep their exchange rate fixed to the U.S. Dollar, they bought up a lot of their currency on forex markets to try to maintain the exchange rate. As investors saw foreign reserves running out, they launched a speculative attack which served as the tipping point that forced the government to devalue.
Investors believe that the government will not maintain a currency peg as it becomes increasingly politically controversial to do so. If they predict this as coming they will launch a Speculative Attack and this will serve as a self fulfilling prophecy of sorts as this makes it even more difficult to maintain a currency peg.
Example The UK was a part of the European Exchange Rate Mechanism (ERM) which pegged the Pound to the German Mark. As Germany raised inflation rates, the UK had to follow suit even though they were in a deep recession and faced rising unemployment. Investors (Jeff loves to name George Soros) bet that the British government would not hold on to the peg as their population suffered. With this expectation they sold billions of pounds in what came to be called Black Wednesday (1992). This worsened the situation and led to the UK abandoning the ERM peg the same day. Private banks borrow foreign currencies (USD for example) but earn revenue in their domestic currency. If the value of their domestic currency goes down even just a little compared to the USD, their debt quickly becomes almost impossible to pay.Example Thai banks in the 90s borrowed massive amounts of USD because American interest rates were low, they felt secure in the Thai currency peg to the dollar. But when the peg was abandoned in response to a real estate bubble burst, banks and other firms who had borrowed USD suddenly needed way more of the Thai currency than expected to pay back the same amount of USD.
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Generations of Currency Crisis.md

item.title: Speculative Attack
item.description: A speculative attack is a sudden and large selling of an asset that is deemed unlikely to hold its value going forward. The term is most closely associated with foreign exchange markets and the selling of a nation's currency.
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Speculative Attack.md

item.title: Black Wednesday (1992)
item.description: Black Wednesday was a Speculative Attack on the Great British pound that occurred on September 16th, 1992. Fears over the Brit's ability to to deal with the ongoing recession while under ERM restrictions led to mass sell offs of the pound sterling that were further enabled by the liberalized capital controls of EMU stage 1.
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Black Wednesday (1992).md

item.title: Index
item.description:
Forewarning This is not a complete list. It is actively being updated and will continue to be until the exam on Wednesday March 25th. If you find any incorrect info, or simply have a suggestion to improve clarity send me an email at harrison.velentzas@gmail.com or dm me on instagram @harry_v06. Alternatively (and preferably if you are familiar with GitHub) you can find the GitHub repository for this site here. Read the README regarding how best to make commits. Below are lists of my notes by category
Graphs
Definitions
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Index.md

item.title: Graphs
item.description:
These are my notes regarding graphs we need to know for the midterm exam. Ideally this will work like a Wikipedia where you can click on links to get to a definition, graph, or other page. I try to be as thorough as possible while also keeping it simple. I do my best to use both the jargon we need to know while also including common wording (typically in parentheses).World Trade in the HO Model
World Trade in the Ricardian Model
Demand Diagonals
Price Level in Bilateral trade
Tariff Graph
Quota Graph
Trade-Diverting PTA
Trade Creating PTA
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Graphs.md

item.title: The Impossible Trinity
item.description:
The Impossible Trinity The impossible trinity is a theory regarding exchange rates and monetary policy. It is called the impossible trinity because, while countries want all 3, the laws of economics make it so that only 2 out of 3 are possible at any one time.Each side of the triangle represents what economies want. Everybody (in theory) wants: Monetary independence Gives them more control in managing their domestic economy Exchange rate stability Creates certainty for business conducting cross-border deals. They can be certain they will be paid at the same value because the currency will not increase/decrease in value unexpectedly changing their return on investment. Capital mobility to enable foreign savings and investment Capital Mobility & Monetary Independence Cannot have exchange rate stability Why? adjusting interest rates (monetary policy) and allowing capital to flow across your border freely, will result in capital rushing into and out of your economy as it hunts for the most favorable interest rate, in doing so the exchange rate Monetary Independence & Exchange Rate Stability Cannot have capital mobility Why? keeping your currency at a fixed price (exchange rate stability) while also setting your own interest rates (monetary independence) means that you cannot allow people to trade your currency freely on the open market as that would cause Exchange Rate Stability & Capital Mobility Cannot have monetary independence Why? If you allow money to flow freely in and out of your economy and you refuse to let the exchange rate change your interest rate must be matched with a country whose currency yours is pegged to.
Lecture 5 slides 58-60
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/The Impossible Trinity.md

item.title: Production Possibilities Frontier
item.description: The production possibilities frontier is a graph representing all possible quantities of products that may be produced when all factors of production are used with perfect efficiency.The X and Y axis each represent the quantity produced of a given commodity. The graph displays the production capacity of one commodity for any given production level of the other.Any point beneath the curve is inefficient. Any point beyond the curve is impossible.The slope of a PPF at any given point is called the Marginal Rate of Transformation. In other words it is the marginal "Opportunity Cost" of a commodity. It measures how much of good Y is given up for one more unit of good X or X for Y. Outward shift = growth in input availability or technological progress Inward shift = contraction in input availability
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Production Possibilities Frontier.md

item.title: Definitions
item.description:
Forewarning This is not a complete list. It is actively being updated and will continue to be until the exam on Wednesday March 25th. If you find any incorrect info, or simply have a suggestion to improve clarity send me an email at harrison.velentzas@gmail.com or dm me on instagram @harry_v06. Alternatively (and preferably if you are familiar with GitHub) you can find the GitHub repository for this site here. Read the README regarding how best to make commits. These are my notes on assorted we need to know for the midterm exam. I try to be as thorough as makes sense possible while also keeping it simple. I do my best to use both the jargon we need to know while also including common wording (typically in parentheses).
Production Possibilities Frontier
Consumer Surplus
Producer Surplus
The Impossible Trinity
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Definitions.md

item.title: Producer Surplus
item.description: Producer Surplus is the difference between the minimum price that producers (those who manufacture or otherwise sell a good or service) would be willing to sell at, and the market price that is higher than that minimum.
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Producer Surplus.md

item.title: Trade-Diverting PTA
item.description:
It may be beneficial to first recall Trade Creating PTAs
Trade-Diverting Preferential Trade Agreement effect on Brazil Key: *ZB = Brazilian imports, ZBPTA = Brazilian imports with PTA, PA = Price in Argentina, PS = Prince in El Salvador, T = Tariff (the domestic price in Brazil under autarky is found at the intersection of SB and DB)Assume Brazil currently has tariffs on both Argentinian and El Salvadorian imports. El Salvador in this case has the lowest cost for this product. Trade diversion occurs when Brazil makes a PTA with Argentina, who has a higher cost. Before the PTA (i.e. with equal tariffs) it is expected that Brazil was trading with El Salvador because PS + T was the lowest price available. But with a PTA between Argentina and Brazil, Brazil will begin to trade with Argentina instead. This is the trade being diverted from El Salvador to Argentina because they are subject to different tariffs.
In this graph Brazil is gaining A, B, C, and D as Consumer Surplus. They are loosing A as Producer Surplus. They also loose both C and E which was the tariffs from El Salvador that they are no longer collecting because trade with El Salvador (theoretically) ends for this product. The net gain is B + D, but you will notice that those are quite small so E (the larger area) outweighs it. This results in a net loss from this trade diversion that does not happen with trade creation.
Lecture 2 slides 50 & 51
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Trade-Diverting PTA.md

item.title: Trade Creating PTA
item.description:
This note will not reexplain the Tariff Graph and will assume you are reasonably well versed in it
Trade Creating Preferential Trade Agreement effect on Brazil I have added colors here to make the shapes more readable Key: ZB = Brazilian imports, ZBPTA = Brazilian imports with PTA, PA = Price in Argentina, PS = Prince in El Salvador, T = Tariff (the domestic price in Brazil under autarky is found at the intersection of SB and DB)*Assume Brazil currently has tariffs on both Argentinian and El Salvadorian imports. In this case Brazil would like to trade with Argentina because, even with tariffs, they have the lowest price. Reducing or removing tariffs on both countries equally would lead Brazil to still trade with Argentina, though the volume (amount) traded will increase due to the lower price. This is trade creation by removing tariffs with a preferential trade agreement.
The Consumer Surplus gained is shapes ABCD. It is NOT effected by El Salvadors price level with or without tariffs. This is because the lower bound of prices for this product is always determined by the lowest price, which El Salvador does not have in either case.**Gains and loss for the Brazilian economy by signing this PTA A
Gain: Consumer Surplus
Loss: Producer Surplus B
Gain: Consumer Surplus Loss: none C
Gain: Consumer Surplus Loss: Government tariff revenue D
Gain: Consumer Surplus Loss: none Net welfare is a gain of B + D
Lecture 2 slides 46-49
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Trade Creating PTA.md

item.title: Quota Graph
item.description:
It may be beneficial to first recall the Tariff Graph
Effects of a quota on Japan's imports of rice *Key: PW = World Price, Pquota = Price with quota, ZJ = Japanese rice imports, ZJquota = Japanese imports with quota, QS = Quantity supplied without quota QD = Quantity demanded without quota, QSquota = Quantity supplied with quota, QDquota = Quantity demanded with quota (all in terms of rice)Rather than directly imposing a price increase like a tariff does by adding a tax on imports, a quota limits the quantity which may be imported. But ultimately the effect is nearly the same in terms of the graph. The limited supply of cheaper foreign goods drives prices up. Quotas are regulated by licensing to either foreign suppliers or domestic importers
The remainder of this note will look very similar to Tariff Graph. Key differences will be emphasized with ALL CAPS
When the price is at PW, shapes A, B, C, and D are all part of Consumer Surplus.When the price is at Pquota:
A: Producer Surplus domestic producers regain the ability to sell to individuals who are willing to purchase rice at this higher price
B: Deadweight loss C: DEPENDS ON IMPLEMENTATION OF LICENSING If domestic importers get the licensing for foreign goods they gain C If foreign suppliers get the licensing they gain C
D: Deadweight loss He emphasized this a lot in lectures
When a quota is imposed the price shifts from PW to Pquota. In the process, areas B and D are lost and not gained by anyone. Hence why they are called deadweight loss Without the tariff this is part of Consumer Surplus.
Suggestion I would suggest consulting the summary of welfare effects of both tariffs and quotas on page 28 of lecture 2
Lecture 2 slides 24-28
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Quota Graph.md

item.title: Tariff Graph
item.description:
Effects of a tariff on Japan's imports of rice Key: PW = World Price, T = Tariff, ZJ = Japanese rice imports, ZJtariff = Japanese imports with tariff, QS = Quantity supplied without tariff QD = Quantity demanded without tariff, QStariff = Quantity supplied with tariff, QDtariff = Quantity demanded with tariff (all in terms of rice)
When the price is at PW, shapes A, B, C, and D are all part of Consumer Surplus. When the price is at PW + T:
A: Producer Surplus domestic producers regain the ability to sell to individuals who are willing to purchase rice at this higher price
B: Deadweight loss C: Revenue for the government by collecting the tariff
D: Deadweight loss He emphasized this a lot in lectures
When a tariff is imposed the price shifts from PW to PW + T. In the process, areas B and D are lost and not gained by anyone. Hence why they are called deadweight loss. Without the tariff this is part of Consumer Surplus. The Net Welfare Effect (N), can be represented by the following equation.
Suggestion I would suggest consulting the summary of welfare effects of both tariffs and quotas on page 28 of lecture 2
Lecture 2 slides 20-23
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Tariff Graph.md

item.title: Price Level in Bilateral trade
item.description:
Rice trade between Japan and Vietnam The price levels for rice in both economies (PV for Vietnam, PJ for Japan) will converge towards the world price (PW). This will have a different effect in each country. In Japan: The price of rice will fall (decrease) Benefiting Japanese consumers of rice, who will pay a lower price for rice. Hurting Japanese producers of rice, who will be unable to compete with cheaper Vietnamese prices and thus sell less rice. Graphically
Japanese producers will loose area C (Producer Surplus)
Japanese consumers will gain area C and area D (Consumer Surplus) The Japanese economy sees the net gain of area D (this is the gain from trade for Japan) In Vietnam: the price of rice will rise (increase) Benefiting Vietnamese producers of rice, who will sell more rice by undercutting (selling at a lower price) Japanese producers Hurting Vietnamese consumers, who will pay a higher price for rice Graphically
Vietnamese producers will gain area A and area B (Producer Surplus)
Vietnamese consumers will loose area A (Consumer Surplus) The Vietnamese economy sees the net gain of area B (this is the gain from trade for Vietnam) The rule In (bilateral) trade, the economy with the higher autarky price sees a benefit to the consumer, the economy with a lower autarky price a benefit to the producer. The inverse is also true: the economy with a higher autarky price will have its producers hurt, the country with a lower autarky price will have its consumers hurt. The gains to the benefiting party will always outweigh the loss to the hurt party. Thus, the mutual benefits from trade.
Lecture 1 sides 34 & 35
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Price Level in Bilateral trade.md

item.title: Consumer Surplus
item.description: Consumer Surplus is the material gain for consumers (those purchasing a good or service), obtained when they can purchase a product for a price lower than the absolute most they would willing purchase it for.
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Definitions/Consumer Surplus.md

item.title: Demand Diagonals
item.description:
This note is shit I don't fully get Demand Diagonals myself to be honest so feel free to make recommendations for this one because its pretty brief It is best to first recall the Production Possibilities Frontier
DD on the Ricardian Model
In the Ricardian model the PPF is a straight line because it assumes that technology is the only factor influencing the opportunity cost. DD is the Demand Diagonal, it represents the ratio an economy wishes to consume a product for a given quantity of the other product. Using the above example the DD is in effect answering the question "If a consumer in this economy is purchasing 5 units of rice, how many motorcycles are they purchasing?"The takeaway (I guess?) Where the Demand Diagonal intercepts the price line (in autarky this is the PPF) is the quantity that the economy will consume of each product under free trade.
Lecture 1 sides 38 & 45
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/Demand Diagonals.md

item.title: World Trade in the Ricardian Model
item.description:
It may be helpful to first recall Demand Diagonals and Production Possibilities Frontier
World Trade in the Riccardian Model
DD: the Demand Diagonal
Unlabeled solid: the Production Possibilities Frontier in autarky
Dashed line: the Production Possibilities Frontier with world trade Point A represents consumption in autarky
This point is on the Demand Diagonal because that is where the economy is in line with consumer demand Point B represents complete specialization in a single product In these cases that is motorcycles for Japan and rice for Vietnam because that is what they each respectively have a comparative advantage in. Point C represents consumption when each economy trades its excess products to the other
This enables them to both consume beyond their domestic PPF.
This point is on the Demand Diagonal because that is where the economy is in line with consumer demand
Measuring Imports and Exports Z = Imports, E = Exports, CVR = Vietnamese Rice Consumption, CJM = Japanese Motorcycle Consumption, ZVM = Vietnamese Motorcycle Imports, ZJR = Japanese Rice Imports (his graph labels kinda suck ass here so bear with me)In the above graph we assume that each country is producing at point B (see previous section). Vietnam will consume rice until point C (CVR), Japan will consume motorcycles until point C (CJM). All additional product, not consumed domestically, is then exported (represented by EJM and EVR respectively). Since each country is putting all if its resources into its most efficiently produced product (producing at B), they import all of their consumption of the other product. For Vietnam this means their consumption of motorcycles is entirely imported (ZVM). For Japan this means their consumption of rice is entirely imported (ZJR).
Lecture 1 sides 43-52
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/World Trade in the Ricardian Model.md

item.title: World Trade in the HO Model
item.description:
It may be helpful to first recall Demand Diagonals, Production Possibilities Frontier and World Trade in the Ricardian Model
World Trade in the HO Model Z = Imports, E = Exports, EVR = Vietnamese Rice Exports, EJM = Japanese Motorcycle Exports, ZVM = Vietnamese Motorcycle Imports, ZJR = Japanese Rice Imports
The Heckscher-Ohlin (HO) model operates effectively the same as the Ricardian model except the PPF is determined in a more realistic (and by extension complex) manner. The HO model has a curved PPF because (amongst other factors) it becomes incredibly difficult to use every resource for rice production instead of motorcycle production (Ex. how do you use a welding torch for rice cultivation instead of making a motorcycle?).
For this reason, point B is no longer a country putting ALL of it's resources towards the production of the product it has comparative advantage in. Instead it finds the most efficient level of production based on the world price of the product. B is plotted where the world price line (the dashed one) intersects the PPF.But why does Vietnam import a minority of its motorcycles when Japan is still importing most of it's rice? If you look at the curve of the PPFs you might notice that Japan's is sharper than Vietnam's. This represents that Japan has a higher opportunity cost for producing rice instead of motorcycles. They produce vastly more motorcycles than rice for the same amount of resources. Vietnam on the other hand has a more balanced opportunity cost. Even when producing at point B (most efficient based on the world price), they still have enough resources to fulfill most of their motorcycle demand. The remainder (ZVM) is then imported. When Japan produces at it's point B it has practically no resources left for rice rice production, meaning it then has to import the majority (ZJR)
Lecture 1 sides 53-64
item.guid: 200 - Education/Leiden University/Semester 4/Global Political Economy/Webnotes/Graphs/World Trade in the HO Model.md
